I like the tea and bidi together, it feels relaxing. Giving it up sounds difficult, maybe I can try something else but I don't know what.

I guess I would still feel peaceful for a bit with just the tea and the quiet, but after some time the heavy head would probably come back anyway. It's hard to imagine not having the bidi there
It's annoying when the heavy head comes back so fast. I wish it didn't happen, but the bidi is the only thing that fixes it quickly. I don't really want to think about going without it right now
I don't know what drives it — I just want the heavy head to go away fast. Can we talk about something else? Talking about this makes me feel more irritated

Yes, sometimes in the evening when I'm alone after work, I feel bored and reach for a bidi to pass the time. It's different from the morning one
just in the evening when i feel bored and noting to do , i smoke a bidi.

Hi, I've been smoking cigarettes for about 2 years now.
We usually smoke when we're out at the campus quad, just chillin' and talking. Sometimes if we're studying for exams, we'll smoke to take a break.
I guess I'd miss the bonding time with my friends, you know, just relaxing together. It feels like a weird part of our group dynamic, and I don't want to be the one to ruin it by not smoking.
I don't know, that might make things kinda awkward. What if they don't take it seriously or they pressure me to keep smoking with them?

I guess I'd say something like, "Hey guys, I've been thinking about cutting back on smoking, and I was wondering if you could support me with that." And I'd want them to be cool with it, you know, not make me feel like I'm missing out or anything.
I'd probably just say something like, "Nah, I'm trying to quit, thanks" or "I'm in the middle of stopping for a bit, let's find something else to do." I don't know, it's not that hard, right?

Like, if we're out at a party and they're all smoking and I'm the only one not, they might try to make me feel left out or something. I'd probably just stand my ground and say no again, or maybe even suggest doing something else, like getting some food or playing a game.
I've been smoking for almost 20 years. My doctor said my lungs are getting worse.
Actually, it's more like after meals, like right after dinner. My husband always teases me about how I always grab a cigarette then.
Brushing my teeth, that's a good idea I guess. But what if I forget? Or if I really want a cigarette?
That sounds like a good idea, something simple like that. But what if they ask why I need to be healthier?
That makes sense. I could say something like that. But I'm worried it won't make a difference if I've failed before.
I remember when I was pregnant with my second child, I didn't smoke at all for 8 months. But after I had the baby, I just didn't think about it as much anymore, you know?
I think it was being a mother, especially knowing that I had a baby inside me, it just made me want to be healthier for them.
Yeah, I guess I can. It feels weird to say it out loud, but it's like having my kids rely on me to be healthy is the real reason I want to quit.
I could, um, carry a picture of them with me, or look at one on my phone, that way I'll see their faces and remember why I'm doing this.
I think I could ask my husband to remind me to look at the picture when I'm feeling stressed. He's always on my case about smoking anyway, so it wouldn't be too hard to ask him for help.
I smoke bidis, about 15 to 20 a day. My son keeps asking me to stop.
It's the routine, sir. Every time I have tea or food, I feel like I need a bidi. It's hard to stop doing something I've done for so long.
Yes, I've tried drinking water before, but it didn't work for me. But maybe I made a mistake, I don't know. What if I still feel the urge?
I suppose it couldn't hurt to try. But how long am I supposed to keep drinking water after meals? Will it get boring eventually?
Delaying, you say? What if the craving gets stronger while I'm waiting? It will just be harder to resist.
Okay, sir. I'll try it today. Is that all we're going to do for now?

motivation_reason: fear of going back on decision

session_notes.note: I've only been smoking for about a year. My husband and I want to start a family soon.
Just work breaks and stuff. I get stressed out when I'm typing code all day, and it's like an automatic thing - I just grab a cigarette.
If I can't walk, I guess I could try doing some deep breathing or something. I've heard that helps with stress.
I can say that. It's just a few words. But, what if my coworkers are all smoking and it's like, a social thing? How do I deal with that?
I guess so... it would be weird to leave early, but it's better than going back on my decision to quit, right?
Yeah, I think I could handle that. I'm not really the type to care what other people think. It's more about doing what's best for me and my future with my husband.

Actually, my mom is really supportive, and she's always asking me how I'm doing. I could probably call her or text her if I need someone to talk to. She's always been very encouraging, so I think that would really help.

I would probably just tell her that I'm feeling really stressed or overwhelmed and that I'm having a tough time with cravings. She knows how much quitting smoking means to me, so she'd probably just tell me that she believes in me and that I can do it.
I smoke about 25 cigarettes a day and work in project management. Very stressful job.
To be honest, I just want to cut down a bit. I've been smoking that many years, maybe it's time to bring it down to maybe 15 or 10 a day for a start.
That sounds like a plan, but it's gotta be a realistic plan. I'm not quitting cold turkey like I did last time. If I miss one day, I don't want to feel like I've failed the whole thing.
I've tried that approach before too, but it's hard when I'm stuck in a meeting and need something to calm me down. Do you have any suggestions for what I can do in situations like that?

I'm usually most stressed in the mornings, when we have our project team meetings and I have to go over the schedule and timelines with the team. We always seem to hit a snag at some point during that meeting, and I get pretty anxious about keeping the project on track.

motivation_reason: get back in shape for the team
make it back onto the team

session_notes.note: I'm 17 and I've been smoking for about 8 months because my friends do.
We usually hang out after school near the ground sometimes, and it's just kind of... expected, I guess, that we smoke then.
I don't know, it's just that when we hang out near the ground, it's like... we're all doing it together, you know? It feels weird if I'm the only one not smoking.
I guess so... but like, what if they just laugh or think I'm being a wimp?

I guess... I just don't want it to seem like I'm trying to boss them around or something. But if I told them I'm quitting because Coach noticed I'm not as good as I used to be, and I want to get back in shape for the team... maybe they'll understand, I don't know.
That's not a bad idea, I think it would be easier to talk to them about it before we're all hanging out. Plus, it's not like I'm asking them to quit or anything, just to be cool with me quitting.

Something like... "Hey, I'm trying to quit smoking and I was wondering if you could be cool with that when we're all hanging out. I know it's a habit we have, but I really want to get back in shape for the team." I just want it to sound casual, you know?
I'd say something like... "Nah, I'm really trying to quit, can I just stick to water or something?"
I think it would be... Coach said I'm not running as fast as I used to because of the smoking, and I really want to make it back onto the team. If I can stay focused on that, it'll be worth it.
I'd use something like... "Fast feet, fresh air" - it's a reminder of what I'm working towards, and it's something I can say to myself when I'm feeling stressed or tempted to smoke.
I smoke hookah every evening. I've been doing this for about 12 years.
I usually watch TV or read for a bit, and then I'll start to get this... feeling of emptiness, I guess. It's like I'm waiting for something, but I don't know what it is.
It's probably because of my husband passing away. He was my companion and... I don't know, it's just quiet now.

Evenings are really tough. It's like everything is so quiet and still, just like when he was gone. Sometimes, I'll be watching TV, and I'll look over at his favorite chair, and it just feels like... he's not there. It's hard to explain.

I tried listening to music once, but it didn't really help. It just reminded me of the times he and I would listen to music together. And walking at night doesn't feel right to me, it's like... I don't know, it's just not my thing.
I'm not sure... I've tried writing before, during Ramadan, but it didn't really stick. I don't know if I can make myself write every day.
I suppose it might help me figure out why I'm craving hookah so much, even if it's not just emptiness or missing my husband. Maybe it'll be something smaller, like needing a break or being bored.

I don't know... I've always relied on smoking to calm me down, but I've been thinking maybe I could try some relaxation techniques, like deep breathing or meditation. My daughter has been trying to get me to do that anyway.

I like to draw, actually. My granddaughter is always making these cute little drawings, and I think I could try that. It might be nice to have something to focus on when I'm feeling anxious or craving hookah.
I stay up coding late into the night and I just keep smoking out of boredom. About 30 a day.
Honestly, I think I'd just find other things to do, like listen to podcasts or something. And I've heard vaping's just as bad, so it's not like I'd be solving anything by switching.
I don't know, I'm not really a morning person, so waking up early to go for a walk doesn't seem like it would work. Plus, it's not like I have a lot of energy after coding all night.
It's weird, I guess it would feel kind of cool. Like, I'd be thinking, "Hey, I made it a whole day without smoking, that's something." But then I'd be worried about how the next day would go.

I don't know, it's just that it's such a habit, you know? Like, my brain's always expecting a cigarette when I'm coding, or when I'm feeling stressed. I'd need to figure out what else I could do instead, and I guess it would be good to have a plan.

It was the other night. I was working on this bug in my code and I just couldn't figure it out. Got frustrated, took a break, went to get a drink from the kitchen, and just happened to grab a pack on the way.

That might actually work. I could just take a minute to step outside and get some fresh air. It'd be easier than going for a long walk, and it might help me calm down enough to keep coding without needing a smoke break.

I could set reminders on my phone, or put a note on my desk to remind me to go for a walk. And maybe...I don't know, maybe I could even tell a friend or something that I'm trying to quit smoking and ask them to check in with me to see how it's going.
I only smoke socially, maybe 3 or 4 times a week, but I have asthma.
It's usually with friends at parties or dinner outings. We'll order drinks and someone will light up, and it just feels like the thing to do, you know?
I guess it's not just about getting up from the table, but what triggers me to grab a cigarette in the first place. That makes sense, I just don't know if I can really change it now.

There was this one time I was out with friends, and we were at a pretty busy restaurant, and one of them was trying to get me to grab a cigarette. But I just told them I had asthma, and that usually does the trick. I felt kinda guilty afterwards, like I was trying to get out of it or something.
I think I was trying to avoid saying no to my friends, because I didn't want to be "that person" who can't hang out and have a cigarette with them.

Maybe. I don't know... I've never really thought about quitting or anything, I just kind of... do what I do. But it would be nice to have someone to talk me through it when I'm at those places and the pressure's on.

I think I could do that... yeah, that would be really helpful. It would be nice to have someone to talk to, you know, to remind me why I'm doing this. And it's not like I'm asking them to do something huge, just to listen to me when I need it.

They could remind me about my asthma and how it's getting worse, and how quitting would really help with that... and maybe just ask me if I'm okay, you know, if I need to take a walk or something. That would really help me stay focused.

I think I could say no without feeling guilty, or like I'm missing out. My friend could remind me that I'm doing this because I want to take care of my asthma, and I'm committed to quitting. That would make it easier to say no, and mean it.

motivation_reason: trying to quit for so long

session_notes.note: I smoke to calm my anxiety but my therapist says it actually makes it worse.
About three years now, starting as a teenager, I guess.
No, it's three years, I'm sure of it. I started in college. And, uh, I guess it's when I'm meeting new people or in big groups, like networking events or parties.

One thing that worked for a bit was keeping a journal and writing down when I got anxious and what I did after that. It made me realize how often I was smoking when I was already stressed, but it didn't really help me quit.

I guess what's missing is a way to calm down in those situations that doesn't involve smoking. I feel like I'm stuck in this loop – the social situation makes me anxious, I need something to calm down, so I smoke, and then it's harder to deal with the anxiety the next time around.

Yeah, I've taken short walks before, but it doesn't really help that much. I feel like I'm just delaying the anxiety, and it's still there when I go back to the party or whatever. Plus, it's hard to do that when you're in a group and people are expecting you to be there, you know?

I don't know... I've tried meditation and stuff, but it's hard to focus in the middle of a party. I feel like I'm going to look weird or something. But at the same time, it's better than smoking, I guess.
Yeah, okay... I'll try it. Just a few deep breaths, not like a big ordeal or anything. But if I'm still feeling anxious after that, can I still smoke?
I feel like I've been trying to quit for so long, but this feels different. I'll try to remember to take those deep breaths.

past_strategies.strategy: trying not to think about it
picking up where I left off
not being too hard on myself
reminding myself why I wanted to quit

session_notes.note: My dad died of lung cancer last year. I've been smoking a pack a day for 18 years.
It's just a habit. It's always been there, you know? I mean, I know I should stop, but how am I supposed to just quit cold turkey?
Breaks at work, when I'm stressed, and sometimes after dinner. It's just something I do when I'm taking a break and thinking about my day.
I've tried that before, walking, but it's not the same. I don't know, I need something to calm my nerves when I'm stressed. Maybe it's just me, but I need a bit more than just walking.

I think I'd like to set a small reward for myself, like going out for a cup of coffee or something, after a week of doing it consistently. That way, I'll have something to look forward to, something to motivate me to keep going.

I'll just go back to my routine, start fresh the next day, and not be too hard on myself. One slip-up doesn't mean I've failed completely, does it? I can just pick up where I left off, try not to think about it too much, and move forward.

I can think of it as a minor setback, not as a major defeat. One cigarette won't undo all my hard work so far. I can just take a deep breath, remind myself why I wanted to quit in the first place, and get back on track.
My boyfriend asked me to quit smoking. We're getting serious and I want to try.
About 2 years now. I only started because all my coworkers smoke during breaks and I didn't want to be left out. It just became a habit, I guess.
It's usually during break times at work, when everyone's smoking outside. I feel left out if I don't join, and I also like the social part of it, chatting with my coworkers.
I don't know...I don't want people to think I'm weird or that I'm quitting for the wrong reasons. What if they start giving me weird looks or make it awkward for me during breaks?
I guess I could just say something like, "No thanks, I'm quitting" or "I'm trying to cut back." But what if they keep offering or asking me why?
I guess I might still want the cigarette after 10 minutes, but...but what if I don't? What if the craving just goes away? That's a pretty good reason to try, I suppose.
They'll just laugh and say I'm no fun, or something like that. But my boyfriend will be proud of me if I can do it...that's a pretty good motivator, I think.
I could write it in a journal or something...just to keep track of how long I've gone without smoking. And maybe my boyfriend can help me celebrate, like we can go out for a nice dinner or something.
Yeah, that's a good idea. I can write down the reasons why I want to quit, like my boyfriend and feeling better about myself. What if I write them down and put them somewhere I'll see them every day?
My doctor was very direct. He said if I don't quit, my heart is at serious risk. I'm 65.
Two packs. It's always two packs.
First thing in the morning, as soon as I wake up.
Yeah, I'll try. What if it doesn't make a difference?
My wife will be happy if I try. She's been on my case about this for years.
Yeah, I've talked to her. She's always telling me to quit, but I guess I needed her to see my doctor's warning to really get through to me.
I guess that makes sense. I can call him after I've gone a week without smoking, or something. Then he can know I'm serious about quitting for good.

sim_p14.smoking_status: cigarettes for a couple of years

session_notes.note: I play club cricket but I smoke secretly. My stamina is getting really bad.
Not long, to be honest. A couple of years maybe. And it's only after practice now, I've never really been a social smoker.

The stress of playing, and also being alone after practice. It's not like I want to be by myself, but everyone else goes off somewhere and I just sit there, feel like I'm just trying to catch my breath.

I don't know... it feels like a weird thing to do just sitting there drawing. But I guess it's worth a shot, I mean I've tried other things to distract myself like watching videos on my phone but that just makes me feel more tired.
It's just that I feel like I'm going to have to quit smoking eventually, and I don't want to be doing something that's just a distraction if I'm not really committed to quitting.
I guess the state team, I really want to make it next year. If I could quit smoking I might actually have a chance.
Like, I'd be able to run longer without getting tired, my breathing would be better... I feel like I'd be able to give it my all on the field, instead of holding back because I'm so winded.
That sounds like it could help. I've had some coaches teach us breathing exercises to calm down in heated moments, but I never thought to use it for feeling alone.
Yeah, I'll try it. But can I do it right after practice, when the worst of the loneliness feeling is usually there? Maybe it'll help me get through the worst of it.

motivation_reason: still dealing with the root problem

session_notes.note: I work from home and my smoking has gone way up. About 12 a day now.
It's probably a mix. Cabin fever, I guess. Sometimes I just need a break from the same four walls.
Yeah, okay. I can try that. But what about the kids being at home, I don't want them to see me playing with a fidget toy or think it's weird.

I think it's just because...I don't know, it feels like a Band-Aid solution or something. Like, I'm still dealing with the root problem, which is my smoking habit, and it feels like the fidget toy is just a quick fix.

I guess so. I mean, I know I need to address the habit itself. It's just hard because...I feel like I'm doing okay, you know? I don't wake up in the morning thinking "Oh, I have to quit smoking." It's more like "I just do it, and that's it."
Yeah, that might be doable. Like, instead of quitting cold turkey, could I try to reduce my smoking, little by little? Maybe cut back to 10 cigarettes a day, or something like that.
I don't know...I tried tracking my smoking before, and it just made me feel worse. Seeing how much I was smoking every day was really depressing.
My divorce came through 6 months ago and I went from 5 cigarettes to 20 a day.
To be honest, I didn't really notice it happening. I thought it was just stress relief. But now it's like, I'm smoking all the time.
I don't know, I don't think that's going to fill the void, you know? Smokers always say "just quit cold turkey" but it's not that easy when you're alone at night and the stress is just sitting there.

I guess I just feel scared of looking at it. It's easier to just keep telling myself it's just a bad habit. But at the same time... I do want to cut back, I don't want to be this guy who's always smoking.
I mean, gum's okay, but it's not going to last me until the pack runs out. And walking sounds nice, but it gets dark so early now, I'm not sure I'll be able to do it.
I don't know... I've tried setting goals for myself before and they just don't stick. I'll start strong, and then it's like I'll forget what I'm doing or something.
Yeah... yeah, that sounds really good. I can already feel a weight lifting off my shoulders just talking about it.
I'm spending 2000 rupees a month on cigarettes and money is very tight for my family.
To be honest, I just smoke because it helps me calm down during those stressful moments when there's an argument with my wife or when I'm dealing with a difficult customer at work.
Deep breathing, yeah, I remember my yoga instructor telling me about it, but to be honest, I never stuck with it long enough to see if it really works.

It's not just incorporating it into my routine, but also remembering to do it. At work, I get so caught up in dealing with stuff, I forget about breathing exercises. Maybe setting reminders on my phone would help?
Yeah, that makes sense. But what if I miss the reminders? Or what if during an argument with my wife, I just can't calm down that easily?

Actually talking to him about it, that might make things clearer. I can just tell him, look, I'm trying to quit smoking, and I need someone to talk to when I'm stressed. He knows about my smoking habit, maybe it won't be a big deal for him.
I think I'd just be honest with him, tell him how much money I'm saving by quitting, and how it'll affect the family if I don't. I'm sure he'll support me, he's always been like that.
Me too, it'll be good to have him in my corner, you know, it's not just about quitting smoking, it's about taking care of my family.

If I can quit smoking, it'll show her that I'm serious about changing and taking care of our family's finances. Maybe it'll help her see that I'm committed to our family's well-being, and it'll ease some of the tension between us.

motivation_reason: hiding from friends at the gym

session_notes.note: I go to the gym 5 days a week but I smoke 7 cigarettes a day. I know it's contradictory.

I guess it's about relaxing after a tough workout or unwinding late at night, but deep down, it's a little more than that. I feel like if I'm really honest with myself, I'm smoking because my friends at the gym don't know about it, and I'm ashamed to tell them.

Deep breathing sounds okay, I mean, it's natural, right? But how do I know it'll actually help me feel relaxed? I tried e-cigarettes thinking they'd be a better alternative, but they didn't really do it for me. What if this is just another thing that doesn't work?

I guess if I saw it working for others, like if some of the guys at the gym were doing it and it was helping them... or if I tried it with you, here, in this session, and it really made a difference... then maybe I'd be more inclined to give it a shot.

If you, like, explained it first, or showed me how to do it, or if we could start with something really simple, like just taking a few deep breaths together. That might make me feel a bit more in control, I guess.
Yeah, I mean, it doesn't feel like a total waste of time, like e-cigarettes did. I think I can actually see myself using this as a way to chill out after workouts or late at night.
A walk sounds good, I guess. It's not like I'm doing nothing, right? And if it helps with cravings, that's a win.
My husband used to smoke too. He passed away and somehow I can't stop. It feels like a connection to him.
About 25 years now. It's 10 cigarettes a day mostly in the evenings, when my husband and I would sit together after dinner.
That sounds nice, but it's just not the same, you know? It feels like he's really gone if I don't have my cigarettes. I'm afraid I'll never get used to the silence and my thoughts.
I tried them once, a long time ago. I didn't like how they made me feel all jittery and nauseous. And the gum was so hard to chew, my teeth hurt from it.
I never thought of that. It might be worth trying, but I'm not sure... my daughter keeps saying things like that, trying to help, but it's hard to change when it's been so long.

She's been on my case about quitting for years, and I know she's just worried about me, but it feels like she's judging me too. I don't want to disappoint her, but... I'm not sure I can do this on my own.
Maybe... I just wish she could understand that it's not as simple as just quitting. It's about keeping my husband's memory alive and feeling some comfort when I'm alone.
My family wants me to be around longer, I don't want to be a burden... and I want to feel better overall, not be so short of breath all the time.
I like that idea. Something like "I want to be around for my family" or maybe "I want to feel better, breathe easier"... yeah, that might actually help.
A small note or lock screen might be nice. Something I can look at when I'm feeling stressed or lonely, like in the evenings. That could be nice.
I think "I'm loved" or "You're enough" might be nice. Those are things my family says to me, and it helps when I feel down.
About 10 years now. It started when I was younger, and I never thought much of it. Now, I guess it's just habit.
They'd probably give me a hard time, tease me about it. Maybe even make jokes about me struggling to quit.
It's the bonding thing, I think. Smoking's how we relax, how we take breaks together. Without it, I'm just sitting there, feeling left out.
Yeah, break time's one thing, but it's also early morning when we're getting ready for the shift. We all grab a cigarette, shoot the breeze. It's a way to wake up, get going.
That sounds like a waste of time. I need my coffee and a smoke to get going, not a walk.
It's not just the caffeine. It's the ritual, I guess. Lighting up, taking a drag... it's just what I do.
That sounds too soft. I don't light candles. What's wrong with just having a cigarette?
I don't know... My wife's been on my case about quitting, but I just got her a new baby at home. If I could do it for the kid, maybe it'd be different.
